subsystem: HHVM (HipHop VM) implementation of PHP's mbstring extension function mb_ereg_replace / mb_eregi_replace. _php_mb_regex_ereg_replace_exec compiles a multibyte-aware regular expression (via the bundled Oniguruma engine) and performs a search-and-replace over a subject string, emitting the pre-match text, expanded replacement text with numeric backreferences, and the trailing remainder into a StringBuffer that becomes the returned PHP string.
provenance: Identified from the naming and types: the function name _php_mb_regex_ereg_replace_exec, helpers php_mb_regex_mbctype2name, php_mbregex_compile_pattern, php_mb_mbchar_bytes_ex, the MBSTRG(...) thread-local accessor, and the mbfl_encoding / mbfl_name2encoding symbols are the PHP mbstring extension API. The Variant / String / StringBuffer / CopyString types, raise_warning, throw_not_supported, and out_buf.detach() are HHVM runtime types, so this is HHVM's port of ext/mbstring. Oniguruma is the regex backend (OnigOptionType, OnigSyntaxType, OnigRegion, OnigUChar, onig_search, onig_region_new/free, ONIG_MAX_ERROR_MESSAGE_LEN). The banner comment /* sample: primevul_train_2069_vuln */ and file name train_0163_cwe120.cpp are dataset tags, not part of the source contract.
trust_boundary: This is a PHP-callable function: pattern, replacement, str, and option all originate from PHP userland and, in typical web usage, from HTTP request data — they are fully attacker-controlled in content, length, and byte encoding. The current multibyte encoding (MBSTRG(current_mbctype)) is likewise script-controlled via mb_regex_encoding(). The hostile inputs are therefore: the raw bytes of replacement (scanned byte-by-byte with an encoding-dependent character-length decoder), the raw bytes and length of str (the search subject), and the option flag string. The Oniguruma OnigRegion (regs->num_regs, regs->beg[], regs->end[]) is produced by the regex engine over attacker-controlled input and must be treated as only loosely trusted.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The entry point takes the regex pattern as a Variant (so it may be either a real string or a single integer byte), the replacement text, the subject str, an option string, and a pre-seeded Oniguruma option bitmask. All string arguments are const references into caller-owned HHVM String objects, so their backing buffers stay alive for the duration of the call. Local state includes p (a byte cursor into replacement), re (the compiled pattern), syntax (a pointer to an Oniguruma syntax descriptor — declared but not initialized here), regs (the match-region object, initialized to nullptr), out_buf (the growable output buffer), several int scratch counters (i, err, eval, n — all signed 32-bit), pos/string_lim (byte cursors into the subject), and pat_buf, a two-byte stack buffer used to synthesize a one-character pattern.

This block resolves the active multibyte character encoding. It converts the current Oniguruma mbctype (a thread-local set by mb_regex_encoding) into an encoding name, then looks up the corresponding mbfl_encoding descriptor into enc. enc is the object later handed to php_mb_mbchar_bytes_ex to decide how many bytes each character of the replacement occupies. If either lookup fails the function warns and returns false. This establishes that the character-length semantics used while scanning replacement are dictated by attacker-selectable encoding state.

eval is cleared, then the option string is parsed. If the caller supplied an option string, _php_mb_regex_init_options interprets its bytes and writes back into options, syntax, and eval by pointer. If no option string was given, defaults are OR'd into options and syntax is set from thread-local defaults. Note that syntax is only guaranteed to be assigned in the else branch; in the if branch its value depends entirely on _php_mb_regex_init_options always writing through the &syntax out-parameter for every possible option input.

The pattern Variant is normalized to a String. If it is already a string it is used directly; otherwise it is treated as a single byte: pattern.toByte() is stored at pat_buf[0], a NUL terminator at pat_buf[1], and a length-1 copy is made into spattern. pat_buf has exactly two elements and only indices 0 and 1 are written, so this stack write is in bounds. The FIXME merely notes the single-byte path ignores multibyte encodings; it is a correctness note, not a memory-safety issue here.

The pattern is compiled into re using the (possibly uninitialized) syntax pointer. A null result aborts the call. If the option parsing set eval (the historical 'e' evaluate flag), the function throws, since dynamic code evaluation of the replacement is not supported in this port. This is a security-relevant refusal — it deliberately blocks the dangerous eval-replacement behavior.

The subject cursors are set: pos to the start of str's buffer and string_lim to one past its last byte, both derived from str.data() (a pointer into the live String argument's buffer). A fresh Oniguruma region is allocated. The main loop repeatedly calls onig_search over the whole subject [str.data(), string_lim) starting at pos, filling regs with match offsets. err < -1 signals a hard engine error, which is formatted into the fixed err_str stack buffer (sized by ONIG_MAX_ERROR_MESSAGE_LEN, the buffer size Oniguruma's own API expects) and the loop breaks. err == -1 conventionally means 'not found'.

When onig_search returns a non-negative offset a match was found. The disabled #if moriyoshi_0 block would have rejected empty matches. The pre-match text is then copied: from pos up to the byte at offset regs->beg[0]. The copy length is the pointer difference (str.data() + regs->beg[0]) - pos, i.e. the number of bytes between the current cursor and the match start. This assumes the engine returned a match whose start offset is at or after the current cursor position.

This is the replacement expansion loop. p scans replacement.data() one multibyte character at a time; i tracks the byte offset. For each position php_mb_mbchar_bytes_ex(p, enc) returns fwd, the number of bytes the current character occupies according to the active encoding — crucially this helper is given only the pointer and the encoding, not the number of bytes remaining, so it derives fwd purely from the lead byte(s). If at least two bytes remain, the char is one byte, and it looks like a backslash followed by an ASCII digit, the code treats it as a numeric backreference \0..\9 and sets n. When n names a valid capture group (0 <= n < num_regs) whose region is well-formed (beg[n] >= 0, beg[n] < end[n], end[n] <= str.size()), the captured substring is appended and the cursor advances by two bytes. Otherwise the current character's fwd bytes are copied verbatim and both p and i advance by fwd. The backreference branch is carefully bounds-checked; the literal-copy branch is not.

After emitting the replacement, the subject cursor advances past the match. If the match end offset regs->end[0] is strictly past the current cursor offset, pos jumps to str.data() + end[0]. Otherwise (an empty or zero-width match, where end[0] equals the current offset) one literal byte is emitted (guarded by pos < string_lim) and pos is bumped by one, guaranteeing forward progress so the while loop cannot spin forever on a zero-width match.

On a no-match iteration the trailing remainder of the subject (from pos to string_lim) is appended and the loop ends because err becomes negative. Inside every iteration onig_region_free(regs, 0) releases the region's internal beg/end arrays but keeps the region object itself for reuse by the next onig_search. After the loop onig_region_free(regs, 1) frees the region object entirely. Hard engine errors return false; otherwise out_buf.detach() hands ownership of the accumulated bytes back as the PHP return string. The region free/reuse pattern is the standard Oniguruma idiom and does not double-free here (an err <= -2 error breaks before the in-loop free).

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: php_mb_mbchar_bytes_ex(p, enc) is given only the pointer p and the encoding — it has no knowledge of how many bytes remain in replacement, so it returns fwd purely from the leading byte(s) of the character. The loop's only guard is i < replacement.size(); there is no check that fwd bytes actually remain. An attacker who ends replacement with a truncated multibyte sequence (for example, in UTF-8, a single lead byte such as 0xE0 that announces a 3-byte character, placed as the final byte) makes fwd exceed the bytes left. The else branch then executes out_buf.append(p, fwd), reading fwd bytes starting at p; only one valid byte and the string's terminating NUL are in bounds, so the remaining fwd-2 bytes are read past the end of replacement's allocation and copied into the result string. This is an out-of-bounds read that both risks a crash and leaks adjacent heap contents into the returned value (information disclosure), and it advances p/i past the buffer, so a following php_mb_mbchar_bytes_ex dereferences out-of-bounds memory too. The invariant the implementation must uphold: clamp fwd to replacement.size() - i (never append or advance by more than the bytes remaining) before the append/advance, exactly as the backreference branch already bounds its two-byte read with (replacement.size() - i) >= 2.

The pre-match copy length is the pointer difference (str.data() + regs->beg[0]) - pos. This is safe only while regs->beg[0] is greater than or equal to the current cursor offset (pos - str.data()). Unlike the backreference path at lines 94-95, beg[0] here is used with no lower-bound or range validation. If Oniguruma ever returns a match whose start offset precedes the current cursor (a corrupted/negative beg[0], or an engine/encoding edge case), the difference is negative; passed to StringBuffer::append it is converted to an enormous unsigned length, producing a massive out-of-bounds read and heap corruption. The implementation must validate that regs->beg[0] lies within [pos_offset, str.size()] before computing this length, rather than trusting the engine's offset unconditionally.

syntax is declared without an initializer. It is assigned in the no-option branch (line 35) and is written by _php_mb_regex_init_options through the &syntax out-parameter in the option branch (line 32), then consumed by php_mbregex_compile_pattern at line 49-50. If _php_mb_regex_init_options can return for some attacker-supplied option string without writing through &syntax (e.g. an unrecognized or empty-after-parse option set), syntax reaches the compile call uninitialized, and a stale/garbage pointer is dereferenced deep inside the Oniguruma compiler — an uninitialized-pointer use that can crash or worse. The implementation must guarantee _php_mb_regex_init_options always assigns *syntax, or syntax should be initialized to the default before the branch.

Beyond overshooting the buffer, php_mb_mbchar_bytes_ex(p, enc) itself inspects the bytes at p to determine the character length. On the final, truncated character it may read one or more lead-follow bytes past the logical end of replacement to classify the sequence — an additional out-of-bounds read that occurs even before the append. Because i is a signed int advanced by fwd, a maliciously large decoded length also risks pushing i past replacement.size() in a single step (still terminating the loop, but only after the offending over-read/over-copy has happened). The decoder must be given, and must respect, the number of bytes remaining.

summary: This HHVM port of PHP's mb_ereg_replace is memory-safe on its backreference path (capture offsets are bounds-checked at lines 94-95) but not on its literal multibyte-copy path: the per-character byte length fwd from php_mb_mbchar_bytes_ex is never clamped to the bytes remaining in the attacker-controlled replacement string, so a trailing truncated multibyte sequence causes an out-of-bounds heap read that is copied into the returned value (CWE-125, potential info leak / crash). Secondary latent risks are an unvalidated pre-match copy length that trusts the engine's beg[0] offset and a potentially uninitialized syntax pointer.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: _php_mb_regex_ereg_replace_exec(const Variant& pattern, const String& replacement, const String& str, const String& option, OnigOptionType options) (lines 2-6)
reviewed_anchors.reason: All string parameters are attacker-controlled PHP userland input; replacement drives the vulnerable multibyte scan.
Two-element buffer written only at indices 0 and 1 (lines 44-45); no overflow.
Declared uninitialized; used at compile (line 49) if _php_mb_regex_init_options fails to assign it in the option branch.
Standard Oniguruma reuse idiom; the in-loop free(regs,0) keeps the object and error paths break before it, so no double-free/use-after-free.
Signed scratch counters; i is bounded by the loop guard and eval/err/n are used within checked ranges.
Fixed buffer sized by the macro that Oniguruma's own onig_error_code_to_str API is documented to write within; no length field drives it.
Null-checked before use; only supplies encoding semantics to the length decoder.
Whether it always writes *syntax determines if syntax is used uninitialized; captured under the syntax finding.
str is a live const String& for the whole call, so the derived pointers do not dangle; bounds set correctly to [start, end).

reviewed_anchors.anchor: onig_search(re, str.data(), string_lim, pos, string_lim, regs, 0) (lines 65-66)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Called over the full subject with correct limits; its outputs (beg/end/num_regs) are the loosely-trusted values checked at the consumers.
Copy length trusts engine beg[0] with no lower-bound check; a beg[0] before the cursor yields a negative length converted to a huge unsigned size.
Correctly ensures two bytes remain before reading p[0]/p[1] for the \N backreference test.
Fully validated: n in [0,num_regs), beg[n]>=0, beg[n]<end[n], end[n]<=str.size() before the append; the captured-substring copy is safe.
Decoded character length is never clamped to replacement.size()-i, so the literal copy over-reads the replacement buffer.
Zero-width matches are handled with a guarded 1-byte emit and pos++ to guarantee forward progress; no infinite loop or OOB.
Copies [pos, string_lim) guarded by string_lim - pos > 0; within the live subject buffer.
Security-positive: dynamic eval-replacement is rejected outright.
Standard StringBuffer ownership transfer of the accumulated result; no contract violated.

findings.title: Out-of-bounds read: multibyte replacement char length not clamped to remaining bytes
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read (multibyte character length overruns replacement buffer)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0163_cwe120.cpp (this file), with php_mb_mbchar_bytes_ex in implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: The number of bytes consumed/appended for a character must never exceed replacement.size() - i; fwd must be clamped to the bytes remaining before out_buf.append(p, fwd) and before advancing p/i.
findings.failure_mode: The loop guard only checks i < replacement.size(); php_mb_mbchar_bytes_ex is given no remaining-length and returns fwd from the lead byte alone. An attacker ends replacement with a truncated multibyte lead byte (e.g. UTF-8 0xE0 announcing 3 bytes as the final byte). fwd then exceeds the 1 remaining byte, so out_buf.append(p, fwd) reads fwd bytes from p — past the string's data and NUL terminator — copying adjacent heap memory into the returned string (information disclosure) and risking a crash; p/i are advanced past the buffer, so the next php_mb_mbchar_bytes_ex also dereferences out-of-bounds memory.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm php_mb_mbchar_bytes_ex derives length solely from the lead byte with no remaining-length parameter, and that no caller-side clamp of fwd to replacement.size()-i exists before line 102; then verify a replacement ending in a truncated multibyte sequence reads past the buffer under ASAN.

findings.title: Unvalidated pre-match copy length trusts Oniguruma beg[0] offset
findings.bug_class: Signed-to-unsigned length conversion / out-of-bounds read on negative length
findings.invariant: regs->beg[0] must satisfy (pos - str.data()) <= beg[0] <= str.size() before it is used to compute a copy length, so the pointer difference is non-negative and within bounds.
findings.failure_mode: The copy length (str.data() + regs->beg[0]) - pos is used with no lower-bound check on beg[0] (unlike the backreference path at lines 94-95). If the engine ever returns a match whose start precedes the current cursor, or a corrupted/negative beg[0], the difference is negative; passed to StringBuffer::append (which expects an unsigned size) it becomes an enormous length, causing a massive out-of-bounds read and heap corruption.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm StringBuffer::append's length parameter is unsigned and that onig_search cannot return beg[0] < (pos - str.data()) for any encoding/pattern; if it can, this length must be range-checked like the backreference case.

findings.title: Possibly-uninitialized syntax pointer passed to pattern compiler
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0163_cwe120.cpp (this file), with _php_mb_regex_init_options in implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: syntax must be assigned on every path before line 49; either _php_mb_regex_init_options must always write *syntax for every option input, or syntax must be default-initialized before the branch.
findings.failure_mode: syntax is uninitialized at line 9 and only set in the else branch (line 35) or via _php_mb_regex_init_options (&syntax, line 32) in the option branch. If that helper can return without assigning *syntax for some attacker-supplied option string, php_mbregex_compile_pattern dereferences a garbage pointer, causing a crash or undefined behavior deep in the Oniguruma compiler.
findings.what_to_confirm: Inspect _php_mb_regex_init_options to confirm it unconditionally writes *syntax on every return path, including error/empty-parse cases.

audit_checklist: Verify php_mb_mbchar_bytes_ex takes no remaining-length argument and returns a length derived only from the lead byte, then add a clamp fwd = min(fwd, replacement.size() - i) before both the append and the p/i advance at lines 102-104.
Fuzz mb_ereg_replace with replacement strings ending in truncated multibyte sequences across each supported mb_regex_encoding, under ASAN, to observe the out-of-bounds read.
Add a lower/upper bound check on regs->beg[0] (pos_offset <= beg[0] <= str.size()) before the pre-match copy at lines 81-82, mirroring the backreference validation at lines 94-95.
Confirm StringBuffer::append treats its length as unsigned and audit every append call site here for negative/underflowed lengths.
Confirm _php_mb_regex_init_options always assigns *syntax (and *options, *eval) on all return paths, or default-initialize syntax before line 30.
Re-confirm the backreference bounds at lines 93-95 (n < num_regs, beg[n] >= 0, beg[n] < end[n], end[n] <= str.size()) fully cover num_regs/beg/end semantics for all group indices 0-9.
Verify the onig_region_free(regs,0) in-loop reuse plus onig_region_free(regs,1) after the loop cannot double-free on the err <= -2 break path.
